section.title: Haman's Downfall
section.sectionRationale: At the second banquet the trap finally springs. When the king renews his open-handed offer, Esther at last names her petition: her own life and the life of her people, sold out to destruction. Pressed to say who would dare such a thing, she points to Haman. The king storms out in fury; Haman, pleading for his life, falls across Esther's couch just as the king returns, sealing his doom. Harbonah mentions the gallows Haman built for Mordecai, and Haman is hanged on it. The plotter dies by his own device.
section.thesis: A brave queen speaks, a proud enemy is unmasked, and the gallows built for the righteous receives the wicked instead, the hidden God turning the plot back on the plotter's own head.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Esther's petition and the naming of the enemy; the king's fury and Haman's desperate plea; and the reversal by which Haman hangs on his own gallows.

verses.text: So the king and Haman went to dine with Esther the queen,

and as they drank their wine on that second day, the king asked once more, “Queen Esther, what is your petition? It will be given to you. What is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be fulfilled.”
Queen Esther replied, “If I have found favor in your sight, O king, and if it pleases the king, grant me my life as my {{sheelah|petition}}, and the lives of my people as my request.

For my people and I have been sold out to destruction, death, and annihilation. If we had merely been sold as menservants and maidservants, I would have remained silent, because no such distress would justify burdening the king.”
Then King Xerxes spoke up and asked Queen Esther, “Who is this, and where is the one who would devise such a scheme?”
Esther replied, “The adversary and enemy is this wicked man, Haman!” And Haman stood in terror before the king and queen.

units.label: The king's fury and Haman's plea
units.note: the proud man begging for his life

verses.text: In his {{chemah|fury}}, the king arose from drinking his wine and went to the palace garden, while Haman stayed behind to beg Queen Esther for his life, for he realized that the king was planning a terrible fate for him.

Just as the king returned from the palace garden to the banquet hall, Haman was falling on the couch where Esther was reclining. The king exclaimed, “Would he actually assault the queen while I am in the palace?” As soon as the words had left the king’s mouth, they covered Haman’s face.

units.label: Hanged on his own gallows
units.note: the plot returns on the plotter

verses.text: Then Harbonah, one of the eunuchs attending the king, said: “There is a gallows fifty cubits high at Haman’s house. He had it built for Mordecai, who gave the report that saved the king.” “Hang him on it!” declared the king.
So they hanged Haman on the {{ets|gallows}} he had prepared for Mordecai. Then the fury of the king subsided.

sheelah.gloss: A petition, a request, the thing asked for. Esther has been invited three times to name her sheelah, and now she does: not treasure or rank, but life itself, her own and her people's. The word frames the whole scene as an appeal for mercy to the only earthly power who can grant it, and the reader senses a greater petition being answered behind the throne.

chemah.gloss: Burning anger, hot wrath, fury. The same royal chemah that once fell on Vashti now blazes against Haman and finally subsides only when he is dead. In a book where God is never named, the king's uncontrollable rage becomes, without anyone intending it, the instrument that removes the enemy of God's people.

ets.gloss: A tree, wood, timber; here the wooden gallows or stake Haman raised for Mordecai. The word ties the ending back to the beginning of Haman's rage: the very ets he built to display his triumph becomes the place of his death. Scripture repeatedly warns that the one who digs a pit falls into it, and here the pattern is exact.

basic.context: This is the chapter where the trap Esther has been setting finally closes. At the second banquet the king again asks what she wants, offering half the kingdom. This time she speaks plainly: someone has plotted to destroy her and her whole people. When the king demands to know who, she names Haman, sitting right there at the table. The king storms out in a rage; Haman, terrified, throws himself on Esther's couch to beg for his life, and the king walks back in to find him there. That seals it. A servant points out the gallows Haman built for Mordecai, and the king orders Haman hanged on it.
basic.hermeneutics: The central question is: who is really running this scene? There is no miracle, only a courageous queen, a hot-tempered king, and a proud man's panic. Yet the outcome is exact justice, the enemy destroyed by the very device he built. The key questions are why Esther waited until now to speak, and whether Haman's death on his own gallows is simply irony or something more. The lesson is that the God who is never named still turns the schemes of the wicked back on their own heads.
basic.originalLanguages: Esther finally names her sheelah, her petition, and it is not for wealth but for life, her own and her people's. The king's chemah, his burning fury, the same rage that once removed Vashti, now falls on Haman and only cools when he is dead. And Haman hangs on the ets, the wooden gallows he built for Mordecai. Three plain words carry the reversal: a plea for life, a royal fury, a gallows turned back on its maker.
basic.typology: Haman, the accuser of God's people who is destroyed on the very wood he prepared for the righteous, is a dark foreshadowing by contrast. The accuser is thrown down, and the one marked for the gallows is spared, a pattern fulfilled when Christ disarmed the accuser at the cross. The wood raised to kill the innocent becomes the place of the enemy's defeat.
basic.christ: Haman is the accuser and enemy who is cast down, and Mordecai the condemned man who is spared. This points forward to the cross, where the true Accuser, Satan, was defeated, and where the innocent One took the place of death so that the guilty might live. The gallows that ruined Haman anticipates the tree on which our enemy was finally overthrown.
basic.god: God is not named once in this chapter, yet His fingerprints are everywhere: the timing of the banquet, the king's rage, the servant who just happens to know about the gallows. The wicked man falls into the pit he dug for the righteous. The book is teaching us that a hidden God governs even the fury of kings to protect His people and to bring justice on their enemies.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 7 says the wicked falls into the hole he has made. Proverbs 26 warns that whoever digs a pit will fall into it. Psalm 37 promises that the sword of the wicked will pierce their own heart. And Colossians 2 shows Christ disarming the accuser and triumphing over him at the cross.
basic.oneStory: The enemy who plotted the death of God's people is destroyed on his own gallows, and the condemned man is spared. It is the gospel shape in miniature, the accuser cast down and the marked one delivered, fulfilled when Christ overthrew the true accuser at the cross and turned an instrument of death into the place of victory.

context.literary: Chapter seven is the swift, violent payoff of a plot built over five chapters. It is tightly framed by the two banquets: the first raised the tension, the second releases it. The scene turns on a single sentence, Esther's naming of Haman, and then accelerates: the king's exit, Haman's fatal collapse on the couch, the servant's timely word, the hanging. The narrator lets the irony do the preaching, for Haman dies on the exact gallows the reader watched him build in chapter five.
context.historical: Persian banquets were conducted on couches, so Haman falling on the couch where Esther reclined would, to a returning king, look like an assault on the queen, a capital offense. Covering the face of a condemned man was a known gesture of doom. Impalement or hanging on a high wooden stake was a real Persian mode of execution, and the fifty-cubit height signals both Haman's earlier arrogance and the public spectacle of his fall.
context.canonical: Haman is called the Agagite, an echo of Agag the Amalekite king whom Saul failed to destroy, so his fall closes an old account in Israel's story. The motif of the wicked destroyed by their own devices runs through the Psalms and Proverbs, and it reaches its climax at the cross, where the powers of darkness were undone by the very death they engineered.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the long-building conflict to its just resolution, showing that the enemy of God's people is unmasked and destroyed by his own scheme, so that the reader trusts the unnamed God to govern the fury of rulers and the outcome of plots, and to vindicate His people at the appointed hour.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a providential reversal and a rightful judgment; it does not prescribe personal revenge or teach a technique for defeating enemies. The abiding truths are God's just governance, His protection of His people, and the moral certainty that the schemes of the wicked recoil on themselves.

debates.question: Is Haman's death on his own gallows presented as divine justice, or merely as narrative irony?

views.label: Providential retribution
views.case: On this reading the exact fit of the punishment to the crime is the point: the hidden God ensures the plot returns on the plotter's own head, exactly as the Psalms and Proverbs promise the wicked will fall into the pit they dig. The Agagite framing, closing Saul's unfinished business with Amalek, strengthens the sense that a moral order beyond Persia is at work. The very device meant to display Haman's triumph becomes the measure of God's justice.

views.label: Literary poetic justice
views.case: Others stress that the narrator never names God here and lets the craft speak for itself, building a perfectly symmetrical irony without an explicit theological claim. On this view the restraint is deliberate: the book invites the reader to supply the theology, refusing to spell out the hand behind the coincidence, so that the ending lands as art before it lands as doctrine.

debates.bearing: Either way the narrative treats the reversal as right and fitting, and the wider canon reads such reversals as the moral governance of God, so the reader is meant to see, behind the perfect irony, the just hand of the God who defends His people and topples their enemies.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on three plain words. It opens with Esther's sheelah, her petition, the request she has been invited three times to make and now finally speaks, and it is not for gold but for life, her own and her people's. It burns with the king's chemah, his hot fury, the very word used when Vashti fell, now blazing against Haman and subsiding only when he is dead. And it closes on the ets, the wooden gallows Haman raised fifty cubits high to shame Mordecai, on which he is himself hanged. A plea for life, a king's wrath, a gallows turned back on its builder: the vocabulary is ordinary, and that is the point, for the God who is never named works through a brave word, a hot temper, and a tall piece of timber to overthrow the enemy of His people.

typology.type: Haman the accuser and enemy, destroyed on the wood he prepared for the righteous
typology.fulfillment: The accuser cast down and disarmed at the cross, where Christ triumphed over the powers on the very tree meant for death, Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: Mordecai, the condemned man spared from the gallows
typology.fulfillment: The innocent delivered from death, foreshadowing the deliverance secured for God's people through the One who took death in their place, Romans 8:33-34.

typology.type: The plot that recoils on the plotter's own head
typology.fulfillment: The final undoing of evil by its own violence, as the powers that crucified the Lord of glory sealed their own defeat, 1 Corinthians 2:8.

study.christ: This chapter shows the accuser cast down and the condemned man spared, and both point to Christ. Haman is the enemy who charges God's people and seeks their death, and he is thrown down and destroyed on his own instrument. Mordecai is the righteous man marked for the gallows, and he is spared. At the cross these threads meet and are fulfilled: the true Accuser, Satan, was disarmed and put to open shame, Colossians 2:15, and the innocent One took the place of death so that the guilty might live. There is no one who can bring a charge against God's elect, for Christ has died and been raised, Romans 8:33-34. The gallows that ruined Haman is a faint shadow of the tree on which our enemy was finally overthrown and our deliverance secured.
study.god: God is not named once in this chapter, yet no chapter is more evidently governed by Him. The second banquet lands at the right moment; the king's fury flares and then, crucially, subsides only after justice is done; Haman's panic drives him to the one posture that dooms him; and a servant appears who happens to know about the gallows and speaks the decisive word. Nothing is labeled the act of God, but the wicked man falls into the very pit he dug for the righteous, exactly as Scripture says he will. The lesson for us is that a hidden God rules even the rage of kings and the timing of a courtier's speech, defending His people and turning the schemes of their enemies back upon themselves.

crossReferences.note: The wicked makes a pit and falls into the hole he has made; his mischief returns on his own head, the exact shape of Haman's end.
Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, the proverb that Haman's gallows enacts to the letter.
Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them, the fulfillment of the accuser cast down.
No one can bring a charge against God's elect, for Christ died and was raised, the deeper deliverance foreshadowed by Mordecai spared.

study.oneStory: At the second banquet the enemy is named, and the accuser who plotted the death of God's people is destroyed on the very gallows he built for the righteous, while the condemned man is spared. It is the gospel reversal in miniature, the accuser cast down and the marked one delivered, and it runs straight to the cross, where Christ disarmed the true accuser and turned an instrument of death into the throne of His triumph.

sermon.title: Haman Is Hanged!
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Esther, this message expounds Esther 7:1-10, Esther's petition and the fall of Haman on his own gallows, tracing the unseen hand of God who works through a brave queen and a proud man's ruin to rescue His people. A faithful, expository handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What did Esther finally ask of the king at the second banquet?

options.text: That her own life and the lives of her people be spared from destruction.
options.note: Esther 7:3-4 shows her petition was for life, not treasure or rank.

options.text: That she be given half the kingdom as the king offered.
options.note: The king offered half the kingdom, but she asked only for life.

options.text: That Mordecai be promoted over Haman.
options.note: Her petition was the survival of her people, not a promotion for Mordecai.

options.text: That she be allowed to return to her homeland.
options.note: She asked for deliverance from death, not for return from exile.

quiz.q: How did the king come to see Haman as guilty of assaulting the queen?

options.text: He returned to find Haman fallen on Esther's couch as he begged for his life.
options.note: Esther 7:8 records the fatal misreading of Haman's desperate plea.

options.text: Esther accused Haman of attacking her directly.
options.note: The king drew the conclusion himself upon seeing Haman on the couch.

options.text: A servant testified that Haman had struck the queen.
options.note: No such testimony was given; the king judged by what he saw on returning.

options.text: Haman confessed to the crime.
options.note: Haman was pleading for his life, not confessing to any assault.

quiz.q: On what was Haman finally executed?

options.text: The very gallows he had built to hang Mordecai.
options.note: Esther 7:9-10 shows the plot recoiling exactly on the plotter.

options.text: A new gallows the king ordered constructed.
options.note: It was the gallows Haman himself had prepared for Mordecai.

options.text: The palace executioner's block.
options.note: He was hanged on his own gallows, the point of the irony.

options.text: A pyre in the city square.
options.note: The text specifies the gallows Haman raised for Mordecai.

quiz.q: What does Haman's death on his own gallows most clearly teach?

options.text: A hidden God turns the schemes of the wicked back upon their own heads.
options.note: The exact fit of punishment to crime reveals God's just governance.

options.text: That clever queens can outwit foolish kings.
options.note: Esther's courage matters, but the lesson is God's justice, not mere cleverness.

options.text: That anger is always a reliable guide to justice.
options.note: The king's rage served God's ends, but Scripture does not commend rage as a guide.

options.text: That God rescues only through visible miracles.
options.note: The opposite: here God works with no miracle at all, through ordinary events.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: The accuser is cast down and the condemned is spared, fulfilled when Christ disarmed the accuser at the cross.
options.note: Colossians 2:15 shows the true accuser overthrown on the tree of death.

options.text: Haman is a picture of the suffering Saviour.
options.note: Haman is the enemy cast down, not a picture of the Saviour.

options.text: The banquet foreshadows the Lord's Supper directly.
options.note: The setting is a banquet, but the christological point is the accuser's defeat.

options.text: The chapter has no bearing on the gospel.
options.note: The reversal of accuser and condemned runs straight to the cross.

questions: Esther waited for the right moment, then spoke the truth even at great risk to herself. Where is God calling you to speak up for others, even when it may cost you?
Haman was destroyed by the very trap he built for someone else. Where might the schemes or resentments you are nursing against another person actually be a pit you are digging for yourself?
The king's fury, an ordinary human temper, became the means of rescue. How does it change your view of God's providence to see Him work even through the moods and tempers of powerful people?
The one marked for the gallows was spared, and the accuser was cast down. How does the assurance that no charge can stand against you in Christ steady you when you feel condemned?
God is never named in this chapter, yet His justice is unmistakable. Where in your own life are you being invited to trust that God is at work even when He seems hidden?

translationNotes.note: The narrator's aside about the one who would devise such a scheme renders a vivid Hebrew idiom, literally whose heart has filled him to do this; the sense is the same, the man bold enough to plot it.
The em dash before Haman's name in the phrase this wicked man, Haman has been rendered as a comma for house style; the accusation lands with the same force.
Fifty cubits is roughly seventy-five feet or twenty-three meters; the towering height underscores Haman's arrogance and the public spectacle of his fall.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Esther 7:1-10. The em dash in verse 6 was converted to a comma per house style; no dashes remain. Sermon verified this session from truthforlife.org (Esther 7:1-10).
